ideas.Man is born free, everywhere in chains:
"Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains." This ringing opening sentence of *The Social Contract* is one of the most famous lines in the history of political thought, and it encapsulates the central problem of Rousseau's philosophy. It expresses at once a lament and a challenge: human beings are by nature free, yet everywhere they live under the constraint, inequality, and domination of existing societies and governments. Why? And by what right?

Rousseau's aim is not simply to protest the chains but to ask what could make them **legitimate**. "How did this change come about? I do not know. What can render it legitimate? That question I think I can answer." The rest of *The Social Contract* is his attempt. He rejects the idea that might makes right, or that some are born to rule others; no one has natural authority over another, and force creates no genuine obligation. Legitimate political authority can rest only on **agreement** — a social contract freely entered into.

The solution Rousseau seeks is a form of association in which each person, though uniting with others, "obeys only himself and remains as free as before." This he finds in the **general will**: by submitting not to any individual master but to the collective will of the community aimed at the common good, citizens give the law to themselves and so remain free even while bound. The chains, rightly forged, become the very condition of a higher, civil freedom. The line thus opens Rousseau's radical reconception of freedom, legitimacy, and popular sovereignty — ideas that helped ignite the French Revolution and shape all modern democratic thought.
ideas.The general will:
The **general will** (*volonté générale*) is the central and most influential — and most debated — concept of Rousseau's political philosophy, the key to his solution of the problem of legitimate authority in *The Social Contract*. It is Rousseau's answer to how people can unite under a common power while each remains free: by having each individual submit not to any other person, but to the collective will of the whole community directed toward the **common good**.

Rousseau carefully distinguishes the general will from the mere "will of all." The **will of all** is simply the sum of private, particular interests — what individuals happen to want for themselves. The **general will** is what remains when one considers only the common interest that citizens share as members of the political body; it wills the good of the community as a whole, not the private advantage of any part. When citizens legislate with this common good in view, they express the general will, and the laws that result are the expression of their own deepest freedom.

Sovereignty, for Rousseau, resides in this general will and belongs inalienably to the **people** as a whole — it cannot be represented or transferred to a king or parliament. This is the foundation of modern popular sovereignty and direct democracy. Yet the concept carries a notorious tension: Rousseau writes that whoever refuses to obey the general will may be "forced to be free," a phrase later critics have read as opening the door to collectivist coercion and even totalitarianism. Liberating or ominous, the general will is one of the most powerful and consequential ideas in modern political thought.
ideas.Natural goodness and corruption by socie:
The claim that human beings are **naturally good** and are made bad by **society** is the moral heart of Rousseau's whole philosophy — a radical inversion of the Christian doctrine of original sin and of Hobbes's grim view of the state of nature. For Hobbes, natural man was violent and selfish, and society a rescue from the "war of all against all." Rousseau reverses this entirely. In the *Discourse on Inequality*, he imagines "natural man" — the solitary savage in the state of nature — as fundamentally innocent, peaceful, and content, moved only by simple self-preservation (*amour de soi*) and a natural **compassion** (*pitié*) for the suffering of others.

What corrupts this original goodness is not nature but **civilization** itself. As human beings came together, developed reason, language, agriculture, and above all **private property**, they fell into comparison, competition, and dependence. From this arose *amour-propre* — a restless, prideful self-love that measures one's worth against others and craves esteem, domination, and status. Inequality, vanity, greed, and cruelty are thus not innate but *social* products, generated by the artificial institutions and corrupting relations of developed society. "Man is born good," Rousseau insists, but "society depraves him."

This diagnosis reverberates through Rousseau's other works and through modernity itself. It grounds his critique of the arts, sciences, and progress as sources of corruption; it shapes his program of **education** in *Emile*, designed to preserve natural goodness; and it inspired Romanticism's exaltation of nature, feeling, and authenticity against the artifice of civilization. Few ideas have done more to shape modern attitudes toward nature, childhood, and society.
ideas.Education according to nature:
Rousseau's philosophy of "education according to nature," set out in his novel-treatise *Emile* (1762), is one of the most influential works on education ever written, and the fountainhead of modern **child-centered** pedagogy. It follows directly from his conviction that human beings are naturally good and corrupted by society: if artificial social influences deform the child, then the aim of a right education must be to protect and cultivate the child's **natural** development, shielding it from premature corruption.

Rousseau's guiding principle is that education should follow the child's own nature and unfolding stages, not force adult knowledge and conventions upon it too soon. "Nature would have children be children before being men." Childhood is not a mere imperfect preparation for adulthood but has its own value and rhythm, and the tutor's task is largely **negative** — not to cram the mind with facts and doctrines, but to arrange the environment so that the child learns through direct **experience**, curiosity, and the natural consequences of its own actions rather than through books and rote instruction. Emile learns by doing, discovering, and encountering the world firsthand; reason and formal learning come only later, once the foundations of character and sound sensation are laid.

Rousseau's ideas — the goodness and distinctiveness of childhood, learning by experience, developmental stages, education of the whole person, and the danger of premature bookishness — profoundly shaped later reformers from Pestalozzi and Froebel to Montessori and Dewey, and much of modern schooling still bears their imprint. (The book's account of the education of girls, by contrast, is far more conventional and has been sharply criticized.) *Emile* remains a foundational text of educational thought.

works.The Social Contract:
*The Social Contract* (*Du contrat social*, 1762) is Rousseau's masterpiece of political philosophy and one of the most influential books ever written on government, freedom, and legitimacy — a work that helped inspire the French Revolution and continues to shape democratic thought. Its guiding question is how legitimate political authority is possible: how human beings, naturally free, can live under law and government without sacrificing their freedom.

The book opens with its famous provocation — "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains" — and sets out to discover what could make the chains legitimate. Rejecting force, conquest, and natural hierarchy as sources of authority, Rousseau argues that legitimate power can rest only on a **social contract**: a free agreement by which individuals unite into a political community. In this act, each person "gives himself to all" and submits not to any master but to the collective body of which he is a member. By obeying the **general will** — the community's shared will directed at the common good — the citizen obeys only himself, and thus, in Rousseau's paradox, remains as free as before.

From this foundation Rousseau develops his radical doctrines: that **sovereignty** belongs inalienably to the people and cannot be represented or divided; that law must express the general will; and that government is merely the people's agent, revocable at their pleasure. Bold, eloquent, and provocative — including the notorious claim that citizens may be "forced to be free" — *The Social Contract* founded modern theories of popular sovereignty, republican self-rule, and democratic legitimacy, and its influence on revolutionary and democratic movements has been immense.
works.Discourse on Inequality:
The *Discourse on the Origin and Foundations of Inequality Among Men* (1755), often called the *Second Discourse*, is Rousseau's most profound work of social and philosophical analysis — a bold conjectural history of humanity that traces how inequality, injustice, and human misery arose. Written for a prize essay competition, it develops the revolutionary thesis at the core of Rousseau's thought: that human beings are naturally good and have been corrupted by the development of society.

Rousseau begins by imagining humanity in the **state of nature**, stripping away everything he takes to be a social acquisition. His "natural man" is a solitary, healthy, self-sufficient creature, neither good nor evil in the moral sense, moved by simple self-preservation and a spontaneous **compassion** for others' suffering. This original condition, though primitive, is one of rough equality, independence, and peace — a direct rebuke to Hobbes's war of all against all.

The heart of the *Discourse* is its account of the **fall**. As humans developed reason, language, cooperation, and settled life, and above all as **private property** emerged — "the first man who, having enclosed a piece of ground, took it into his head to say *this is mine*" — humanity entered a spiral of comparison, competition, dependence, and domination. From property came inequality of wealth and power; from comparison came *amour-propre*, vanity and the hunger for status; and government itself, Rousseau argues, was a fraud instituted by the rich to secure their advantages under the guise of protecting all. Eloquent, radical, and deeply pessimistic about "progress," the *Second Discourse* is one of the founding texts of modern social criticism, influencing Marx, anthropology, and every later critique of civilization.
works.Emile:
*Emile, or On Education* (1762) is Rousseau's great work on education and human development — a hybrid of philosophical treatise and novel that he himself regarded as his most important book. It follows an imagined pupil, Emile, from infancy to adulthood under the care of a tutor, in order to show how a human being might be raised to preserve his **natural goodness** amid a corrupting society, and to become both a free individual and a good citizen.

The work embodies Rousseau's principle of **education according to nature**: that instruction should follow the child's own developmental stages, needs, and capacities rather than impose adult knowledge prematurely. In the early years the tutor's role is largely "negative," protecting the child and letting him learn through direct **experience** and the natural consequences of his actions rather than through books and lectures. Reason, morality, and formal learning are introduced only when the child is ready for them, each in its proper season.

Structured in five books corresponding to stages of life, *Emile* also contains one of Rousseau's most famous and controversial sections, the "**Profession of Faith of the Savoyard Vicar**," a statement of natural religion grounded in conscience and feeling rather than dogma and revelation — which led to the book's condemnation and burning, and forced Rousseau to flee. The final book, on the education of Sophie (Emile's intended wife), offers a far more traditional and restrictive account of women's education that later feminists, beginning with Mary Wollstonecraft, sharply attacked. Despite such tensions, *Emile* transformed Western thinking about childhood and learning, and it stands as the founding classic of modern educational philosophy.
